title: Stroopwafels
subtitle: Dutch waffle sandwiches with a caramel syrup filling
excerpt: Two thin, crisp waffles pressed together around a layer of thick caramel syrup. A Dutch bakery staple made at home with a waffle iron. Set one over a hot mug of coffee and the syrup softens in minutes.
sourceNotes: Stroopwafel is a Dutch confection originating in Gouda in the nineteenth century, attributed to baker Gerard Kamphuisen around 1810. The cookie is documented in Dutch confectionery literature throughout the nineteenth and twentieth centuries as a Gouda specialty. The caramel syrup filling (stroop) distinguishes it from plain waffles.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assembled stroopwafels keep in an airtight tin for up to five days. The waffle discs can be baked a day ahead and filled the following day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: cooked on a waffle iron at medium-high heat; no oven required

glossaryTerms.definition: Sugar heated to between 160°C and 180°C until the sucrose breaks down and develops colour and complex flavour. Beyond 185°C it becomes bitter.

content.text: Stroopwafels are two thin round waffles sandwiched around a layer of soft

syrup. They originated in Gouda in the early nineteenth century and have been a Dutch staple ever since. The traditional way to eat one is to rest it on top of a hot mug of coffee for two minutes so the steam softens the syrup filling before you bite in.

You need a thin waffle iron to make them at home — a Belgian-style deep waffle iron produces something too thick. The waffle discs must be cut into rounds while still warm from the iron and split horizontally to sandwich the filling; this is easiest with a round cutter and a serrated knife, working quickly before the waffles cool and become brittle.

attrs.prepNote: softened, for the waffle dough; plus 100 g for the filling
one sachet; for the waffle dough

content.text: Make and rest the waffle dough

Dissolve {{yeast-dried}} in 80 ml of warm water. Combine {{plain-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{cinnamon-ground}}. Add the dough-quantity {{unsalted-butter}}, {{eggs}}, and the yeast water. Mix to a smooth, fairly stiff dough. Knead briefly for two minutes. Cover and rest for 60 minutes at room temperature.

Heat a thin waffle iron to medium-high. Divide the dough into 24 equal balls, about 25 g each. Press one ball into the waffle iron and cook for 90 seconds until golden. Immediately cut into a round with a 9 cm cutter while still warm, then split horizontally with a serrated knife before it cools. Work quickly: once cold, the waffle will crack rather than split cleanly. Repeat with the remaining balls.
Make the caramel filling and assemble

Melt the filling-quantity {{unsalted-butter}} in a saucepan over medium heat. Add {{dark-brown-sugar}} and {{golden-syrup}} and stir until smooth. Cook for three minutes, stirring constantly. The filling should be thick and glossy; a probe thermometer reading of 115°C indicates it will set to a chewy, spreadable consistency when cool. Spread a generous teaspoon of warm filling on the flat side of one waffle half. Press the matching half on top. Leave to set for 15 minutes before eating.

The caramel must be warm and fluid when spread; it firms quickly as it cools. Keep the pan on very low heat while you assemble. If it sets too firm, add a tablespoon of warm water and stir over low heat to loosen.
A thin electric pizzelle or krumkake iron gives a similar result to a traditional stroopwafel iron if a dedicated iron is not available.
Store in a single layer in an airtight tin; stacked stroopwafels stick together as the filling transfers.
